content:
It’s not what the hype said it would be. It’s stranger, and more useful than that.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Bernd 📷 Dittrich on UnsplashA year ago I started switching to AI-assisted coding.
My argument was simple: AI tools help you remove friction — the small, stupid walls that kill projects not because they’re hard, but because they’re annoying.

CSS you don’t feel like debugging.
A deployment config you’ve never touched.
A dependency conflict that has nothing to do with the idea you were trying to build.
That argument still holds. But a year of daily use has added texture to it. Rougher in some places, sharper in others.
Here’s what I actually learned.
The code agents write can be a mess
I’ll start with the thing no one in the AI-tools marketing wants to say out loud: a lot of agent-generated code is genuinely…
